Title: Fly from DFW to Zurich

Transportation.Description: DFW → Philadelphia → Zurich - Flight was long, but uneventful.

Photos.Caption: Flying to Zurich

Title: Drive from Zurich to Lucerne

Transportation.Description: Beautiful drive to Lucerne. WOW moments all along the way. We arrived in Lucerne about 10:30 AM. We walked the city. Enjoyed the lake and Chappel Bridge. We sat and rested by the carving of the Dying Lion. One of my favorites.

Photos.Caption: Exploring Lucerne
Lake Lucerne views
Lucerne streets
Swiss scenery

Title: Lake Lucerne tour and Mt Rigi

Transportation.Description: Today was very rainy throughout the day. We took a boat/ferry ride across Lake Lucerne to Mt Rigi. We took a Cog Train up to the top of Mt Rigi. The rain turned to snow. IT was very fogging, but it was quite nice. We walked part way down and took the ferry back to Lucerne. We had a great lunch on the Mountain - "Wienerschnitzel" We then climbed to the Lucerne City Wall call "Musegg Wall". It was a surprisingly fun experience.

Photos.Caption: Lake Lucerne
Lake Lucerne
Lake Lucerne
Mt Rigi Cog Train
Mt Rigi Foggy Path
Musegg Wall Lucerne

Title: Lucerne Excursion to Grindelwald

Transportation.Description: Today we drove to my most favorite place in the world. Grindelwald Switzerland!!! I love this place and could definitely live here. We drove from Lucerne to Grindelwald, then to Lauterbrunnen, then to Trümmelbach Falls, then to Interlocken, and then back to Lucerne. We hiked and journeyed all day and are exhausted. We even got some fresh milk, for Lily, from a farm we pasted in Wilderswil at the Zuchtfamilie Flora Milk Farm.

Photos.Caption: Grindelwald
Grindelwald

Transportation.Description: Drive to Zurich, Train to Innsbruck, Drive to Ortisei - This has turned this trip into a Plains, Trains, and Automobiles trip. We have been on an Airplane, Train, Bus, Van, and Boat. Tomorrow Gondola. Ortisei, is beautiful!!!

Title: The Leaning Tour

Transportation.Description: Today we actually slept in a bit and then headed on a tour for Pisa. Wow, this place is beautiful and full of history. The Tower, Cathedral, and Baptistry are all sinking in the loss soil and sand. Funny story, the local university has majors in every science, except... Architecture. Well, I think we can see why. LOL! And then the best dinner of our trip so far. Beef Stew to die for.

Transportation.Description: We had hoped to do a wine tour of Tuscany, but we could never find the right tour. Instead, we went to Piazzale Michelangelo (overlook of entire city), we viewed the Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore, and we went to the Accademia Gallery of Firenze (original David Sculpture). Then we hoped a late train to Rome.

Title: The Team Meetings and Shares Communion on Mars Hill

Transportation.Description: Today we all arrived in Athens. There are 5 from Texas, 5 from Wisconsin, and 2 from Colorado. It is a nice group. We gathered in our AirBNBs and then walked (and trained) to Mars Hill. There we took in the site and then gathered for devotional and communion. It was my pleasure to lead the conversation and communinion tonight.

Photos.Caption: Goodbye to my Little Piece of Heaven (Nafplio)
Still 45 minutes from Athens and the changes are evident. We are heading to big city.
The team is completely together. We journeyed to Mars Hill and I got to teach tonight as well as lead the team in communion. What a blessing.
The view of the Acroplis from Mars Hill. This was most likely the view that Paul had during his sermon.

Title: Soccer with Sudanese Refugees - Only Socks!!!

Transportation.Description: Today we traveled, by train, to the Sudanese Refugee encampment called Malakasa near Sfendali, Greece. It was about a 1 hour trip. This encampment is primarily male refugees looking for temporary or permenate EU passports (their papers). They live in shipping containers and have very little. We played Soccer (futbol) with them for 5 hours. They all have different financial means, which means some have shoes and some do not. So, everyone who played, had to play in only socks to level the playing field. It was a great day. In the morning we climbed the hill and stairs of the Acropolis to see the Parthenon and other Greek ruins.

Videos.Title: The Team and The Refugees - Drills

Photos.Caption: Our team from Wisconsin, Colorado, and Texas. Back Row (left to right): Ellen, Brent, Brent, Josh, Hannah, Zoey, Matt and Bryan. Front Row: Michael, Amelia, and Emma
View of Athens walking up to the Acropolis
The ancient Roman theater built in 161 AD named Odeon of Herodes Atticus. This theater is still used today. If you look close, you can see people working to set up a concernt for tonight.
A view of the back side of the theater with the Parthenon looming over head.

Some kind of fruit that was picked from a tree near where we played soccer today. One of the locals picked it and put it in his hat and handed it out to us. It was delicious. If you know what it was, please let me know.
Some of the Sudanese Refugees listening to instructions on how we are going to play today, along with Josh and Matt from the team.

Title: Playing at Park with Albanian Church Kids and Homeless Walk

Transportation.Description: We had a fantastic day, but it of course came with its challenges. First, we needed to be at Stavros Niarchos Park at 10 AM. The plan was to the Metro train at 9 AM, which we did. As I started investigating, it came to my attention that the 911 bus we needed to take at the end of the train ride does not run on Saturday mornings. So, the options were to take Taxi's, to take multiple buses, or to walk. We collectively decided to walk the 2.7 KM to the park. It was a nice walk and it was early in the morning, so we were still fresh. We met the kids, teens, young adults, and parents of the Porta Albanian Church of Athens. We played kickball, soccer, tag, frisbee, and many other games for the next 4 hours. We ate pizza together and then decided to walk and tour the park and nearby Library. At this point we already had 14,000 steps and getting tired. We walked to the library and then a local who was with us said no problem we will go to the tram to get back to the train station. BTW, it was hot. We walked to the Tram and waited 12 minutes for the tram. It came and was completely packed with people. The locals on the tram had no interest in letting us on and I am not sure we could have put the 10 of us on it. So, we decided to walk the 2 miles back to the train station. Did I mention it was hot. We walked for the next hour and finally got on the train. We made it back to the apartment and Arturo, from the homeless ministry, met us there to guide us on the homeless evening walk. (That is where we take sandwiches to homeless people sleeping on the streets.) So, we walked for another 2 hours. Needless to say, we walked 30,000+ steps.

Photos.Caption: Michael and Brent - Our blister boys. They got significant blisters from playing soccer in socks.
Our team and some of the Albanian Church members.
Homeless man from Afghanistan.
This man had just taken fentanyl and was completely passed out. We thought he might be dead. Our nurse said he had a breathing rate of 5 breaths per minute.
This man is recently on the streets.
This is an Iranian family of 10 living in this little corner.
Someone sleeps on this cardboard and there are 100s of needles in the grass from drug use.
The group I walked with on the first night with a beautiful sunset in Athens.

After our walk, we had dinner in the beautiful restaurant. Everyone of us felt very odd to be eating like this when we were just on the streets giving out sandwiches to people and we are not in this space.
This is an image of a man (father) with his 4 year old and 6 year old children. We all wept at the sight, but I was able to find the beauty in a father protecting his children.
Our team with the Albanian kids.

Title: Worship with Albanian Church and Homeless Walk

Transportation.Description: Today we went to church and worshipped with the Albanians members of La Porta Church in Athens, Greece. We sang and learned and took communion and sang some more and fellowshipped and ate a great meal. We love the Albanian Church and its lovely members. After church we came back to our apartment and had a really nice debrief time with the entire team. A lot of us opened up, some of us cried, we laughed, and got to know each other better. We did our devotional after the debrief time and we realized that our open debrief discussion actually covered the topics of the devo. God is truely GOOD!!! After devo we made more sandwiches and went on another Homeless Walk. We saw some of the same people and some new ones. Last night, I had the pleasure to meet John from England. He and I talked for about 15 minutes. He shared a lot about himself and I was able to pray with him. I was not supposed to go on the homeless walk again tonight, but I wanted to see John again. I wanted him to know that someone wanted to come see him again. I actually got seperated from the team, but I found John on my own. I sat and talked with him for over 45 minutes. He shared so many things about himself and about his journey. He shared some very personal and intimate details. I was so blessed by our time. When I finally had to leave, he asked me to come back if I can, but he understood if I could not. I told him to take care of himself. He said, taking care of himself is easy, the loneliness is not!!! What an image into the lives of the homeless and lost refugees in Athens Greece.

Title: Travel to and Tour Corinth

Transportation.Description:
​Here is a narrative that strings your notes together into a cohesive story of your day.

​Shadows of Empire and the Crossroads of the World ​Standing among the ruins of Corinth today, you can almost feel the heavy, ambitious hand of Rome. The guide reminded us of just how interconnected this ancient world truly was. Take the Roman Colosseum, for instance—an iconic monument funded entirely by the spoils and gold stripped from the Fall of the Jerusalem Temple.

The geopolitical currents of the day ran deep, and Corinth was right at the center of them.

​Because of its unique geography, it became a bustling bottleneck of humanity; nearly all ships and trade routes had to pass through Corinth. It was too valuable a strategic point to leave empty. Recognizing this, Julius Caesar ordered Corinth to be reestablished as a Roman city in 44 BC (Mummius destroying the old Greek city in 146 BC, leaving it a ghost town) remarkably, this was just a few days before his infamous assassination.

Later down the line, Caesar’s influence would stretch into the medical world too—passing a law that if a woman had fatal complications during childbirth, physicians had to cut her open to try and save the child. We still call it a Caesarean section to this day.

​The Stones Cry Out: Echoes of the New Testament

​As we walked the ancient pavement, the pages of the Bible suddenly felt incredibly tangible. The guide stopped us to look at a specific, weathered piece of stone. Inscribed on it was the name Erastus.

In the New Testament, Erastus is mentioned three times—notably by Paul as the city’s treasurer. His name comes from Eros, hinting at a meaning like the one who is loved in that manner, a common naming convention that strongly suggests he was a former slave who rose to high office. Right there on the plaza, the Latin inscription boldly states that Erastus paved this very area using his own money to give back to the city. Seeing it brings new weight to the words of Jesus: If you deny me, even the stones will cry out. The stones in Corinth are indeed crying out, confirming history.

​It hits hard when you contrast Erastus's wealth with the grim economy of his past. In biblical times, the average price of a human being bought into slavery was just thirty pieces of silver. The exact price Judas accepted to betray Christ.

​A Different Kind of God

​The most beautiful part of the day was how the guide contrasted pagan Roman culture with the revolutionary message of the Gospel.

​In the ancient pagan world, statues often depicted a shepherd carrying a sheep on his shoulders. But that sheep was being carried to an altar for sacrifice—to appease a distant, demanding deity. When Jesus uses that same imagery, He flips it entirely. He carries the sheep not to slaughter it, but to save it.

​That shift changes everything. It’s exactly why we love Him. In ancient times, pagans feared, bargained with, or admired Zeus—but they never said they loved him. Love requires relationship, and relationship requires sacrifice from the top down.

​The guide left us with a question that perfectly sums up the wonder of the day, standing at the crossroads of ancient history:

​How many people will die for their Gods? But, how many Gods will die for you?

Videos.Title: Video of Market and Bema Seat in Corinth

Photos.Caption: Our tour guide in Corinth - James. He is an amazing Pauline Scholar. What a joy to see Corinth with him again this year.
Name plate with Erastus mentioned in the June 8th update. This shows and proves that the Christian religion is and was not for the poor alone, it is for us all.
Temple of Apollo

The Bema seat where Gaius (governor of Corinth) over saw Paul's trial. There is so much proof of this event happening. Paul stood right here, and would have been tied to this beating post if he had been guilty. Paul was beatened several times through his life. Every time he was not guilty but he rarely used his Roman citizenship to avoid a beating. Some believe he didn't use his Roman citizenship beacause of the guilt he felt for executing so many Christians.
Josh was asking if he got the sunscreen rubbed in completely - He hadn't.
View of the Gulf of Corinth from where we ate lunch today.
Back in Athens, this is the actual basketball court where Giannis Antetokounmpo grew up and learned to play basketball. He is now an NBA champion and multi-time MVP.

Title: Glocal Refugee Cafe and New Start Trafficing walk

Transportation.Description: Today we started with a fantastic devotional of hearing Tyler's (local Missionary from Jefferson City, MO) testimony. We then moved over to the Glocal Refugee Cafe and helped served over 150 refugees throughout the day. All the men on the team then went to New Start (a ministry trying to free women from sex trafficking and helping to put on the hearts of men to stop engaging in this). we handed out tracks to men walking the streets near the over 200 brothels in the one neighborhood where public brothels are legal. These brothels are called open door. Basically anyone can walk up to the door, push it open and walk in. We stood near some of the entrances and saw several men walk in and shop for their right woman. It was horrible. At these brothels, men only need to spend 10 Euros for every 15 minutes. In Greece, it is honorable to be a male client at a brothel (in some circles) and disgraceful to be a prostitute. There are somethings I guess this American brain will never understand.

Photos.Caption: Inside the New Start office with Dimitrius preparing to go on our first walk.

Almost everywhere you look in Athens, you see graffiti. Right on the edge of one of the most densely populated brothel district in all of Europe is this beautiful mural on the side of the building. You can see from the balconies that this is at least 60 feet high
The roof top areas of the Glocal Cafe for Refugees in Athens.
One of the very rare nice statements of graffiti in the city.
Right in the middle of a very exclusive and expensive shopping district, there is this ancient tiny church.
It is near closing time and the busiest shopping street in Athens is still packed.
We had a ping pong tournament today on the Glocal Cafe rooftop. These are the first, second, and third place winners.
A little fun to end this deep day. We found a statue in Corinth of our fearless leader, Bryan Doyle.

Title: Glocal Refugee Cafe and New Start Trafficing walk - Part 2

Transportation.Description: Today had a similar agenda to yesterday. We served at the Glocal Cafe for Refugees and we went to the New Start ministries for a brothel prayer walk. We ended the day at one of our apartments for a team dinner made by Ty, Brent (Jones), and Michael. They made us a authentic(ish) Mexican Taco dinner. YUMMY!!!! At the cafe we had some great conversations with people from many different countries and had the opportunity to share Jesus with some of them (this is a Swedish Government sponsored program and we are not to proselytize unless they ask us first). We took several of them to a park to play games, have a picnic, and just hang out for 3 hours. After that we headed back over to the New Start Ministries building. Dimitrius shared with us several stories about the brothel industry. There are pictures below that show some detailed information. It is a heavy thing to be a part of and to witness the men going in and out of these brothels. Pray for the women stuck in this industry and pray for the men who have never been introduced to a relationship with Jesus that would convict them of this behavior.

Photos.Caption: The team during our morning devotional.

Brian, Michael, and Brent (Kern) talking with a refugee from Sudan. He was getting close to getting his EU passport and one night while sleeping on the streets, all of his papers were stolen and he is basically having to start over the process.
Handing out new(ish) clothes to the refugees on the rooftop of the Glocal Cafe for Refugees.
Refugees from at least 43 different countries sit around Natalie during some time at the park. They all really loved to draw and color.
A picture of most of the group that went to the park.
A street near Omonia (a section of Athens). There are about 50 brothels within 4 blocks of this picture.

Every red circle represents a location where there is at least 1 brothel and sometimes 4 or 5 in the same multi-story building. The blue #1 is near where our AirBNB apartment is. The blue #2 is near where Glocal Cafe is. There are at least 400 brothels within 4 miles of this location. This is the Victory Square district and it is one of the most populated brothel districts in Europe.
This is another distric near Omonia where there is a large concentration of brothels.
An abstract mural in the Onomia district.

This mural was painted in 2006 when Greece was going through a very difficult time which include national bankruptcy. The story behind the mural is, when people pray we put our hands toether and lift them to God. These are God's hands pleading for the people of Greece to repent and change their ways. This mural is right on the border of one of the largest brothel districts in Europe.

Title: Glocal Refugee Cafe and New Start Trafficing walk - Part 2

Transportation.Description: Today we worked again at the refugee center. I started out by shopping throughout the Omonia and Monastiraki Districts looking for a patio umbrella for the Glocal Refugee Center. I looked in 100s of shops, but had no luck. I did some research online and found a store that looked like they carried this. I call Bryan and he came and picked me up on this car. We drove to Jumbo Store and found the umbrella pictured below. We helped the staff at the center paint a room and we spent most of the day hanging out with refugees. Most of the team visited the Olympic Stadium (the one from the early 1900's). We went to dinner at a nice Chinese food restaurant and then most of the team met at a bar to watch the first World Cup Match.

Photos.Caption: This is the view from Monistiraki Square. You can see the same chapel on the square, Mars Hills up and to the right, and the Acropolis up and to the left.
An active Archeological site in Athens near Montistiraki Square.
Brent K and Ellen after our journey to buy a new Patio Umbrella for the Glocal Refugee Center.
Athens Metropolitan Cathedral - This Church is so pretty and the square is amazing.
Inside the Athens Metropolitan Cathedral.
Inside the Athens Metropolitan Cathedral.
Inside the Athens Metropolitan Cathedral.
